model_info.name: LightGBM Classifier
model_info.description: Gradient Boosting classifier trained on elephant-specific acoustic fingerprints

fingerprint_blocks.name: Rumble Energy
fingerprint_blocks.description: Detailed sub-bands capturing infrasonic structure unique to elephant vocalizations

fingerprint_blocks.name: Call Tremor
fingerprint_blocks.description: Biological vibration pattern from the elephant's larynx during vocalization

fingerprint_blocks.name: Temporal Phases
fingerprint_blocks.description: Onset, body, and offset patterns showing how calls fade in and out

fingerprint_blocks.name: Vocal Timbre
fingerprint_blocks.description: Voice texture differentiating call types like Social vs Alarm rumbles

top_features.name: Decay Rate (frames 28-32)
top_features.explain_detail: This feature measures how quickly energy drops in the last few analysis frames of each call — the shape of the offset rather than the onset.
top_features.explain_why: Elephant social/contact rumbles often taper smoothly, while alarm or protest calls can cut off abruptly. The model leans on decay because context is frequently encoded in release phase dynamics, not only in the steady-state middle of the call.

top_features.name: Tremor Modulation 7.7 Hz
top_features.explain_detail: A narrow-band modulation around ~7.7 Hz reflects cyclic movement in the elephant larynx and soft tissue during phonation — a biological tremor, not random noise.
top_features.explain_why: Tremor strength covaries with arousal and effort. The classifier uses it to separate calm affiliative rumbles from high-intensity or distressed vocalizations that show stronger or more irregular modulation.

top_features.name: Rumble Band 18-25 Hz
top_features.explain_title: Energy in a key infrasonic band
top_features.explain_detail: Sub-band energy between roughly 18–25 Hz sits in the core rumble range used for long-distance social signaling.
top_features.explain_why: Different contexts emphasize slightly different sub-bands within infrasound. This band helps distinguish call types that otherwise look similar in duration or broad spectral tilt.

top_features.name: Attack Slope (frames 1-4)
top_features.explain_detail: Attack slope captures how fast the call ramps from silence (or background) to its working level in the first few frames.
top_features.explain_why: Gradual onsets often match contact or affiliative rumbles; sharper attacks can indicate surprise, protest, or other urgent contexts. The model uses onset shape as an early cue before later frames resolve.

top_features.name: MFCC 2 (brightness)
top_features.explain_detail: The second Mel-frequency cepstral coefficient summarizes broad spectral shape — effectively how bright or nasal the timbre is within the mel filterbank.
top_features.explain_why: Timbre separates callers and call families. MFCC 2 is a compact stand-in for whether energy is stacked low (deep rumble) vs spread upward, which correlates with several behavioral labels in the training set.

top_features.name: Infrasonic Ratio (8-120 Hz)
top_features.explain_detail: Infrasonic ratio compares energy in deep rumble frequencies to the rest of the spectrum used by the model.
top_features.explain_why: Pure infrasonic emphasis is typical of long-range contact rumbles; contexts with more broadband or higher-frequency content shift this ratio. That makes it a strong cue for intent and arousal classes.

top_features.name: Harmonic Regularity 14 Hz
top_features.explain_detail: Measures how regularly spaced harmonic components are around a ~14 Hz spacing pattern in the low band.
top_features.explain_why: Harmonic structure reflects vocal fold vibration and tract filtering. More irregular or smeared harmonics can indicate nonlinear phenomena or effortful calls, which the model associates with specific contexts.

top_features.name: Spectral Contrast Low
top_features.explain_title: Peaks vs valleys in low mel bands
top_features.explain_detail: Spectral contrast compares energy in peaks to valleys across frequency — low-band contrast captures how hollow or peaky the rumble sounds in deep channels.
top_features.explain_why: High contrast can mean sharp formant-like peaks; low contrast can mean filled-in, noise-like rumble. Those textures differ between playful, affiliative, and tense calls, so the model uses this for fine disambiguation.

key_insights.title: How calls END matters more than how they START
key_insights.description: The rate of decay (how quickly the call fades) is the #1 predictor of context. Alarm calls end abruptly while social calls taper gently.

key_insights.title: The 7.7 Hz 'tremor' is a biological signature
key_insights.description: Elephants produce a characteristic vocal tremor caused by laryngeal oscillation. Its intensity varies by emotional state.

key_insights.title: Infrasonic ratios reveal intent
key_insights.description: The balance between deep infrasound (8-120 Hz) and higher harmonics distinguishes contact calls from distress signals.
